title: Why Most Aesthetic Clinics Struggle - And How to Build One That Thrives.

segments.text: Uh so welcome to this podcast. I have got a very successful entrepreneur and doctor with me, Dr. Tristan Meta. He probably doesn't identify publicly himself as very successful entrepreneur, but he is. And he's going to teach us a few things about how you can build a business in aesthetics. We're going to make an announcement about something new that's coming from the clinic that I own in Manchester at the moment. And we are going to also discuss about all the basically struggles that every clinician goes through. Something I've learned, and I'm sure Tristan will know as well because we've been in the industry very similar length of time, coaching, mentoring, training clinicians, is that we all go through so many similar battles. There are often different features and there are many different ways to build a business in aesthetics, which we're going to touch on, but the struggles often share a lot of similarities. So, I'd like to hear a little bit about his struggles. Uh, I'll share any of mine that come up, and there are plenty of those. and uh and then that's going to help you make progress in your journey, which is what I always trying to do for anyone who's listening to our content. So, welcome

Tristan. >> Thanks for having me, Tim. Great to be here. >> Let's just double check that. When did you start in aesthetics? What was your entry into aesthetics? >> Well, I was a very junior doctor. Um I think I was in an F2 doctor at the time, 2013. And one of my I think one of my SHOs's on the ward round explained that they'd just come back from a an injectables course. And I was absolutely fascinated that someone who's in the NHS was already thinking about diversifying away into other forms of the profession. And so uh I looked into I think I was very inspired by that and I started to think about at the same time I was I could tell that I wasn't happy in this traditional route and that for me was uh a combination of just the experience of being on a rotor doing the night shifts all of the things associated with the difficulties of being a junior doctor plus knowing increasingly knowing about my nature and what I think I wanted to do with my

life. Um, I just thought, okay, let's just try this. And so that was 2013. 2013 I did >> foundation year two. >> I was in F2 and >> same exactly the same point as me. That was when I crunch point as well. >> So that was the crunch point. And it's 2013 I did my first kind of basic toxin and filler injectables training day. Um, and you can imagine what that was like. But it was um and then for a couple of years I started thinking about what it is in aesthetics that I wanted to do and you know was it a clinic or was it something bigger than you know what kind of impact did I want to have? 2015 was when I founded Harley Academy. >> So that's already interesting to me because you did something different to what a lot of people do uh which is you asked yourself what do you want to do versus what is everyone else already doing. M >> that is a key part of basically finding yourself in life is asking yourself what do you want

>> versus what do you think you have to do and I didn't do that I asked myself what do I need to do >> to get out of the NHS and I copied everyone else's business model in fact my first business model was >> the mobile model and I copied that from my friend who was doing it because I understood it and I was like okay let's do it >> that obviously makes sense a nightmare >> not today but back then I'm sure it was great >> yeah I mean it did get my name out everyone there were signs boards all over Manchester and everyone did know our name quite quickly, but it was exhausting doing 13 different venues in a day. >> Let's just there's a van in London now driving around with a luxury clinic inside of it. Have you seen that? >> I did see that. Yeah, >> it does look quite luxury. >> I know. I kind of just try that one. >> I was actually in a meeting with a very big manufacturer we both know and love um who encouraged me to do that. Okay. >> I was like very surprised. Oh, it's great. Patients will love it and patients probably will love it. Um I actually don't a lot of people are angry about that ban, but I don't know. I feel as long as you've got a great clinician, well trained and it's a you know a regulated like a nice environment.

environment. >> I actually I applaud the uh the innovation there. >> Yeah, it's definitely innovative. Um but the question you asked is super powerful. I want everyone to ask that which is what is it you actually want to do is very different to what do I have to do? And it and I don't know why I didn't ask it, but over time the pain of making the wrong decision makes you ask better questions. So obviously I went from >> I don't want to be in my car driving around Manchester like to I want to be in one place. So that became the obvious thing and then I found out you know I like training as well. So tell me about that link. How did you how did you move from doing your first training to thinking I'm going to s set up basically a large training opportunity for everyone else? >> I was doing a lot of reading um around business back in my early 20s mid20s and one of the fundamentals of business is what problem do you solve? And the bigger the problem you can solve, the bigger the business you can run. And I think I'm quite

intrinsically driven to have an impact and do something meaningful. And so in the on the spectrum of things that I could have started up in aesthetics, there was a single clinic, there was a clinic chain, there was a device or manufacturing company, something like that. And then there was training. And I think training for me was uh a combination of what I enjoyed doing as a doctor fundamentally. Um really enjoy the process of training and helping people and educating and speaking. Uh I enjoy the structure of it all. um and the progression, but also I find that um it was you know a really important problem to solve in the space and a lot of education that was done 10 years ago and probably even today was very obviously not aligned with the best interests of the patients and so at the same time the government had various reviews. is

it was looking at what should be or what was recommended to be the standard of training and education and that was I was going to these summits in London which was the health education England review and I was learning about what the future was going to look like from a regulatory perspective still hasn't happened um but it was recommended that it would be at a higher education level so then so I thought okay I'm going to start an education company that is predominantly focused on uh higher education. And so I think the DNA of Harley Academy from the beginning was that we are an education company working in the world of aesthetics rather than um I'm a clinician and I'm going to start a training school in aesthetics. And so I think that explains a lot about how we have structured our growth as a business as an education company rather than as me as a specialist who's

teaching how I like to inject. >> And I think so that gives you the bit of the origin story. >> Interesting. >> Yeah. I think that understanding your that's actually something I try and get out of any conversation even with a patient is find out what their philosophy is and then you'll understand the behavior. >> Yeah. Um because we all have a philosophy. It's basically your rules, your hierarchy of things you care about. Um and just switching two of them around completely changes the outcome. So you've switched >> education is above um how you like to inject or uh or even aesthetics. So education first then aesthetics is very different to aesthetics first then education. Completely different outcome. >> Yeah. And building a company um was the priority. um building a company that is does good things and is ethical. Um but then aesthetics was the uh the territory. >> It's interesting you say does good things. Um cuz I think a lot of clinicians battle with how to make money.

money. >> And they sometimes think if you make money, it's inherently not a good thing. >> I don't know if you've met one yet, but I mean there's so many clinicians whose dream is to create a nonprofit, but long before they've created a profit. Yeah. Profit needs to come first. Yeah. learn how to make a profit and then you can make a nonprofit afterwards. But but at the heart of it is believing that what you're doing um if you're making profit can't actually be good. And I really profoundly disagree with that. In fact, I think >> if you look at the most successful people in the world and they always go off and do philanthropy afterwards, they probably do more good with their profit work than they do with the philanthropy afterwards. M like if you Bill Gates is probably a good example. He's trying to do philanthropic work and I know it's controversial but >> will anything he do ever make the world a better more of a better place than allowing people to access computers? Cuz before him it was like putting in little codes on a black and white screen and then he made it accessible to everyone. So everyone could start a business everyone could get information. Unbelievable impact >> then. So so this it's just a that's my

money mindset thing because I've been on a journey with this as well. create something valuable and you'll make money at the end of it, which is what I want aesthetic clinicians to think about. How do you create value for your patients or whatever you want to build in aesthetics? >> And it sounds like that's part of your philosophy as well. Create something valuable and then the money will take care of itself. >> Yes. And I think that we particularly in the UK have this fear of talking about money as clinicians. We're certainly not in a privatized health sector and uh a lot of health care professionals have gone through a very academic program or a very vocational one and ended up in a very caring role and I think business will is such a harsh world. I think you would probably agree with me. A lot of uh a lot of professionals will come into the world of aesthetics and come into the world of business and the rules are extremely different and uh and you have to be prepared for that. And one of the one of

prepared for that. And one of the ways you arm yourself to be prepared for that is to see that money is isn't indifferent. It's not a good thing. It's not a bad thing. It's just an energy. And that in order for us to run a business, you have to drive revenue. And that means that you have to also play the game. And that means you have to have marketing and you have to have sales and you have to have people that manage your finances and tell you about your cash flow and all these things. And uh the bigger the business gets, the more important that becomes. And I suspect a lot of clinicians might have some fear or shame around embracing the fact that you can't get anywhere without the money coming in. And so there is there's a real um limitation there I think that professionals and what I would reassure like you just said as the money will come when you deliver value but also you can be assured yourself that you will as long as your personal values are aligned that you

will do the right thing with that money that comes in within your business and it won't corrupt you. It won't make you become this ruthless commercial person. Um, you've just got to treat it as one of the key functions of a business. >> Absolutely. In fact, um, you know, I was before we went on, I was talking about this survival course I did with Jasper. He's been watching Bear Grills >> survival island and that's what got him into and it got me thinking about profit. Um because what you see on this island when they first get there is they are they have so such limited resources that as soon as they get basically they find a little bit of food they're happy for 24 hours and then it's suddenly a crisis like 24 hours later there's no food. >> Yeah. >> And and it really got me and that's what it feels like >> depending on how much effort they put in to get the reward of food um basically they're either dying or thriving. >> And it hit me that that's basically what profit is. profit buys you time >> to think and to be creative and then to come up with an easier and better way of delivering more value. Basically finding

out >> um more ways of doing things better. So, you know, when there's a bit of time, they create a fishing net and then it's easier for them to create to catch more fish and when they've caught more fish, they have a bit more time to improve their shelter. >> And it's really just humanity surviving better over time. And it happens to be that now you own a piece of it in your business, >> but if you don't build it to have a little bit of surplus at the end of it, you're constantly struggling for your survival. >> And that's incredibly stressful. And >> yeah, >> so thinking about profit as some sort of luxury. In fact, I remember seeing it. There's a shop near where my GP work that put a sign up saying, uh, buy from us. Our profit goes on wages, their profit goes on champagne. And I'm like, the prophet does not go on champagne. But that's the mindset people face. They think, oh, it's like excess and I'm greedy if I've got profit. And actually, profit's just life. Like, it's energy as you said. And >> it takes a while to get it, but once it clicks in your brain that profit isn't

energy for you to basically think it's time for you to and then to innovate and to make things better for people. >> Um, because that's what you do with at least a third of your profit is you're making your business better so that the customers get a better experience. M >> third of it's for a rainy day and then the third of it is to reward you yourself and to build to create in other spheres like your home life which I think is part of what your the process should be. I didn't do that. This this is you should have told me that a long time ago. I one of the reasons I think we've be able to scale and grow the way we have at my own personal expense is because we reinvested all of our profits. you know, I was always I always had in my head the next stage of growth. And so there was always something to spend it on, whether it was people or premises or technology, whatever it was. And so, and I was so um uh eager and aggressive to want to grow the business that I put myself last. And I think I did that a little too long because um as my family will tell you,

my wife and my brother, uh it's taken a long time to turn that into something that's personally rewarding. Um but that is one way to do it. And I think one thing I would love injectors to understand is they have a choice. They can either run a clinic and run it as self-employed, right? And that means that they are the injector. They're doing the treatments. this is the profit, but you're going to kind of cap the people that you employ and you're going to cap the scale of your brand and run it as a lifestyle business. And that that's choice one, which I think is 99% of people. And that the difficult thing is once you've done uh a month of treatments and you find yourself with £5,000, £10,000, whatever it is, it's very difficult. If you've if you've been thinking if your thought was I'm going to move into aesthetics to improve my earning potential and improve my quality of life, it's very difficult to then not put that as a dividend back into your pocket. And uh that's why 99% of

people think, okay, I've made it. This is working. I can now treat my next patient. Uh life is good. All right. And then there's the minority of people that actually want to um then turn that into a business. And I don't I think that a lot of injectors are not um entrepreneurs until they have taken that step into reinvesting uh a significant amount of that profit into what's next and building that and that is that's quite a lonely and risky journey. But that's but that's kind of like the tall narrow peak of the pyramid of people that are building businesses in aesthetics rather than having their own private clinic. >> Yeah, that's interesting. So there's a process there um of well firstly I must correct you I didn't take any money out our business for many years um in fact I lived on my GP salary Miranda got paid nothing and >> so you did it as well basically >> uh so and that framework of like a third is kind of what Coca-Cola used I think um so we're not

running exactly that but I don't I'm also one day you will take what you deserve what you've earned and you should do that because otherwise you'll be demotivated. >> Absolutely. >> To build the next thing. Um and it's uh but it is I think it's useful for people to hear how it actually develops because sometimes you get this idea on social media that you just basically do your training and the next thing you like Louis Vuitton everything. >> It might be for some people but it's on the credit card. >> Um mostly you have to reinvest for a period of time to build a system if you want to build something bigger. But you kind of went through that. There's an idea of firstly what is the business you want to build. Do you want to just treat friends and family when they come or do you want to build a proper business that you can maybe sell to someone else one day? Um or do you want to build something that's scalable? >> Cuz those last two are very different beasts. people choose to make, some don't. The one thing that's really helpful is to think about the money in your business is not your money. Even if you own all of the shares in your company, that money is it doesn't belong to you. It belongs to the company. And uh it's tempting to see your business balance and equate that with your personal one. But if you just draw a really firm line between that and use that money for the business and occasionally draw it out, that that's helpful. In fact, I remember coming home be like, I was only out for an hour and look, I've got £150. >> But obviously, I waited I probably spent

£1,000, waited 3 months for that patient. So, I've actually got £150 for 3 months. >> But anyway, it's exciting to think about how it could play out. Uh, but the sooner you there is something to be said for paying yourself a wage that's enough to cover your hourly rate. So I was just >> I was this magical free person who told himself he was earning a lot but I was putting it all back into the business. If you want to build it around yourself, that's okay. Um, but for many people, in fact, I'd say most people go through a phase of doing that and then they say, "I need someone else to help me." >> And if you haven't considered how much that person will cost, it can also be a painful period. >> So, um, let's talk about what have

you seen from physicians who enter the industry? cumulative annual growth rate and aesthetics has been pegged at around 15%. And that's because of various levels of resistance in the market to these treatments that are slowly being broken through. And so the thing that I think everyone should be optimistic about uh from a market perspective is that we're nowhere near the peak of the market and the uh demand outstrips the supply even today. So even

though astonishingly the volume of people getting trained to inject around the world is very high. levels of training and are expecting them to get rich quick, you know, really quickly and then drop out after one or two months of trying because they're completely unprepared for this. you're in that frame of mind, uh I've never seen anyone that who hasn't done the work and not been successful. you can master that there isn't much competition.
